question_text: A proton moving with velocity v enters a uniform magnetic field B perpendicular to its direction of motion. The path followed by the proton is:
explanation: When a charged particle enters a magnetic field perpendicular to its velocity, the magnetic force F = qv × B acts as a centripetal force. Since this force is always perpendicular to the velocity, it changes the direction but not the speed of the particle. This results in uniform circular motion. The radius of the circle is r = mv/(qB).
topic: Force on Current and Moving Charges
subtopic: motion of charge in magnetic field
subject: cuet-physics

elimination_hints.hint: A straight line would occur only if the velocity were parallel to B (zero force). Here v is perpendicular to B.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking magnetic force does not deflect the particle

elimination_hints.hint: A parabolic path occurs under a constant force (like gravity). The magnetic force changes direction continuously, unlike gravity.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing magnetic force with gravitational force

elimination_hints.hint: A helical path requires a velocity component along B. Since v is entirely perpendicular to B, the path is circular, not helical.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assuming helical motion without a parallel velocity component

question_text: The work done by a magnetic force on a charged particle moving in a magnetic field is:
explanation: The magnetic force F = qv × B is always perpendicular to the velocity of the charged particle. Since work done W = F · ds = F · v dt, and F is perpendicular to v, the dot product F · v = 0. Hence, the magnetic force does no work on a moving charge. It can only change the direction of velocity, not its magnitude.
topic: Force on Current and Moving Charges
subject: cuet-physics

options.text: Depends on the magnitude of charge

elimination_hints.hint: Magnetic force is always perpendicular to velocity. A perpendicular force cannot increase the kinetic energy or do positive work.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assuming any force that acts on a moving particle does work

elimination_hints.hint: Negative work would slow down the particle. But the speed of a charged particle in a pure magnetic field remains constant.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing magnetic force with a drag or resistive force

elimination_hints.hint: Regardless of the magnitude of charge, the magnetic force is always perpendicular to displacement, so work is always zero.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking work depends on charge magnitude alone

subtopic: Lorentz Force and Motion in Fields

question_text: A straight conductor of length 0.5 m carrying a current of 2 A is placed perpendicular to a uniform magnetic field of 0.4 T. The force experienced by the conductor is:
explanation: Using F = BIL sinθ, where B = 0.4 T, I = 2 A, L = 0.5 m, and θ = 90° (perpendicular). F = 0.4 × 2 × 0.5 × sin90° = 0.4 × 2 × 0.5 × 1 = 0.4 N.
topic: Force on Current and Moving Charges
subtopic: force on current-carrying conductor (F=BIL sinθ)
subject: cuet-physics

elimination_hints.hint: This would result from using sinθ = 0.5 (i.e., θ = 30°), but the conductor is perpendicular to the field so sinθ = 1.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using wrong angle in F = BIL sinθ

elimination_hints.hint: This is double the correct answer. Check that you are not multiplying any quantity twice.
elimination_hints.misconception: Arithmetic error in multiplication

elimination_hints.hint: This is one-fourth of the correct value. Verify all substituted values: B = 0.4, I = 2, L = 0.5.
elimination_hints.misconception: Incorrect substitution of given values

question_text: A charged particle moving with velocity v enters a uniform magnetic field B perpendicular to its velocity. The magnetic force on the particle is:
explanation: The magnetic force on a moving charged particle is given by F = qv × B. When the velocity is perpendicular to the magnetic field (θ = 90°), sin 90° = 1, so F = qvB. This is the maximum force the magnetic field can exert on the charge.
topic: Force on Current and Moving Charges
subject: cuet-physics

elimination_hints.hint: The factor of 1/2 does not appear in the Lorentz force formula. F = qvB sinθ has no such coefficient.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing with kinetic energy formula which has a 1/2 factor

elimination_hints.hint: Force is zero only when the charge moves parallel to B (θ = 0°). Here the motion is perpendicular to B, so sinθ = 1.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing perpendicular and parallel motion relative to B

elimination_hints.hint: Dimensionally, qv/B gives C·m/(s·T) which does not have units of force. The correct formula has B in the numerator.
elimination_hints.misconception: Incorrectly placing B in the denominator

subtopic: Lorentz Force and Motion in Fields

question_text: Two long parallel conductors carry currents in the same direction. The force between them is:
explanation: When two parallel conductors carry currents in the same direction, the magnetic field produced by one conductor at the location of the other results in an attractive force between them. This can be verified using the right-hand rule: the field from one wire at the other wire's location creates a force directed toward the first wire. Currents in opposite directions repel each other.
topic: Force on Current and Moving Charges
subject: cuet-physics

options.text: Attractive or repulsive depending on the magnitude of currents

elimination_hints.hint: Repulsive force occurs between parallel conductors carrying currents in opposite directions. Same-direction currents always attract.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing same-direction and opposite-direction current rules

elimination_hints.hint: Two current-carrying conductors always exert a force on each other. The force is zero only when one of the currents is zero.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking parallel conductors do not interact magnetically

elimination_hints.hint: The nature of the force (attractive/repulsive) depends only on the relative direction of currents, not on their magnitudes.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the nature of force with its magnitude

subtopic: Lorentz Force and Motion in Fields

question_text: A proton moves parallel to a uniform magnetic field. The force acting on the proton is:
explanation: The magnetic force on a charged particle is F = qvB sinθ. When the particle moves parallel to B, the angle θ = 0°, and sin 0° = 0. Therefore, F = 0. A magnetic field exerts no force on a charge moving along the field direction.
topic: Force on Current and Moving Charges
subject: cuet-physics

elimination_hints.hint: F = qvB applies only when v is perpendicular to B (θ = 90°). Here the motion is parallel, so sinθ = 0.
elimination_hints.misconception: Ignoring the sinθ dependence in the Lorentz force

elimination_hints.hint: There is no 1/2 factor in the force formula, and regardless the force is zero when velocity is parallel to B.
This expression is dimensionally incorrect for force and does not correspond to any known formula.

subtopic: Lorentz Force and Motion in Fields

question_text: In a velocity selector, electric and magnetic fields are applied perpendicular to each other and to the velocity of charged particles. A particle passes through undeflected when:
explanation: In a velocity selector, the electric force qE and the magnetic force qvB act in opposite directions on the charged particle. For the particle to pass undeflected, these forces must balance: qE = qvB, giving v = E/B. This condition is independent of the charge and mass of the particle, so only particles with velocity v = E/B pass through straight.
topic: Force on Current and Moving Charges
subtopic: velocity selector
subject: cuet-physics

elimination_hints.hint: This is the inverse of the correct relation. From qE = qvB, solve for v: v = E/B, not B/E.
elimination_hints.misconception: Inverting the ratio when solving qE = qvB

elimination_hints.hint: Check dimensions: EB has units of V·T/m, which is not velocity. v = E/B has units (V/m)/(T) = m/s.
elimination_hints.misconception: Multiplying instead of dividing E and B

elimination_hints.hint: This has incorrect dimensions. The balance condition qE = qvB gives a linear relation between v, E, and B.
elimination_hints.misconception: Introducing an incorrect power of E

question_text: An electron (charge e, mass m) and a proton (charge e, mass M) enter a uniform magnetic field perpendicularly with the same kinetic energy. The ratio of radii of their circular paths (rₑ/rₚ) is:
explanation: The radius of circular motion in a magnetic field is r = mv/(eB). Since kinetic energy K = ½mv², we get v = √(2K/m), so r = m√(2K/m)/(eB) = √(2mK)/(eB). Therefore rₑ/rₚ = √(m/M). Since the electron mass m is much smaller than proton mass M, the electron follows a much smaller circle.
topic: Force on Current and Moving Charges
subject: cuet-physics

elimination_hints.hint: This would be the ratio if r were directly proportional to mass (at same velocity). But at same kinetic energy, r = √(2mK)/(eB), giving a square-root dependence.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using r = mv/(qB) without converting velocity in terms of kinetic energy

elimination_hints.hint: M/m > 1 would mean the electron has a larger radius than the proton, which contradicts the fact that the lighter particle has a smaller radius at equal KE.
elimination_hints.misconception: Inverting the mass ratio

elimination_hints.hint: √(M/m) > 1 would imply rₑ > rₚ. Since the electron is lighter, it has less momentum at the same KE and hence a smaller radius.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing which particle has the larger radius

subtopic: Lorentz Force and Motion in Fields

question_text: In a velocity selector, a charged particle passes undeflected when:
explanation: In a velocity selector, crossed electric (E) and magnetic (B) fields are applied. The electric force qE and the magnetic force qvB act in opposite directions. The particle passes undeflected when these forces balance: qE = qvB, giving v = E/B. This condition is independent of the charge and mass of the particle.
topic: Force on Current and Moving Charges
subject: cuet-physics

elimination_hints.hint: B/E is the inverse of the correct answer. From qE = qvB, solving for v gives v = E/B, not B/E.
elimination_hints.misconception: Inverting the ratio while solving qE = qvB

elimination_hints.hint: EB is dimensionally incorrect for velocity. E has units V/m and B has units T, so EB would have units V·T/m, not m/s.
elimination_hints.misconception: Incorrectly combining E and B

elimination_hints.hint: E²/B does not follow from balancing electric and magnetic forces. The correct derivation gives v = E/B.

subtopic: Lorentz Force and Motion in Fields

question_text: A charged particle of mass m and charge q moves in a circular orbit of radius r in a magnetic field B. The kinetic energy of the particle is:
explanation: For circular motion in a magnetic field: qvB = mv²/r, giving v = qBr/m. The kinetic energy is KE = ½mv² = ½m(qBr/m)² = q²B²r²/(2m). This shows that the kinetic energy depends on the square of the charge, field, and radius, and inversely on the mass.
topic: Force on Current and Moving Charges
subtopic: motion of charge in magnetic field
subject: cuet-physics

elimination_hints.hint: This has dimensions of velocity, not energy. Kinetic energy must have dimensions of kg·m²/s².
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing velocity with kinetic energy

elimination_hints.hint: Check the power of r. From v = qBr/m, v² involves r², not r.
elimination_hints.misconception: Missing a factor of r in the derivation

elimination_hints.hint: Check the power of q. From v = qBr/m, v² involves q², not q.
elimination_hints.misconception: Missing a factor of q in the derivation

question_text: In a cyclotron, a proton is accelerated to high speeds. The time period of revolution of the proton in the magnetic field is independent of:
explanation: In a cyclotron, the time period T = 2πm/(qB). This expression shows that T depends on the mass m, charge q, and magnetic field B, but is independent of the speed (or kinetic energy) of the particle and the radius of its circular path. This independence of speed from the time period is the fundamental principle that allows the cyclotron to work with a fixed-frequency oscillating electric field.
topic: Force on Current and Moving Charges
subtopic: cyclotron
subject: cuet-physics

options.text: Speed and radius of the circular path

elimination_hints.hint: T = 2πm/(qB) explicitly contains m in the numerator. Changing the mass changes the time period.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking mass does not affect the cyclotron frequency

elimination_hints.hint: T = 2πm/(qB) contains q in the denominator. Changing the charge changes the time period.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking charge does not affect the cyclotron frequency

elimination_hints.hint: T = 2πm/(qB) contains B in the denominator. A stronger field means a shorter time period.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking the magnetic field does not affect the time period

question_text: A straight conductor of length 0.5 m carrying a current of 2 A is placed in a uniform magnetic field of 0.4 T at an angle of 30° to the field. The force on the conductor is:
explanation: The force on a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field is F = BIL sinθ. Substituting the given values: F = 0.4 × 2 × 0.5 × sin 30° = 0.4 × 2 × 0.5 × 0.5 = 0.2 N.
topic: Force on Current and Moving Charges
subject: cuet-physics

elimination_hints.hint: 0.4 N would be the answer if sinθ = 1 (θ = 90°). Here θ = 30°, so sin 30° = 0.5 must be included.
elimination_hints.misconception: Forgetting the sinθ factor when the conductor is not perpendicular to B

elimination_hints.hint: 0.1 N would result from using sin 30° twice or an arithmetic error. Check: F = 0.4 × 2 × 0.5 × 0.5 = 0.2 N.
elimination_hints.misconception: Arithmetic error in multiplication

elimination_hints.hint: 0.8 N would result from ignoring sin 30° and also doubling the result. F = BIL sinθ, not 2BIL.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using an incorrect formula

subtopic: Lorentz Force and Motion in Fields

question_text: A charged particle moves in a helical path in a uniform magnetic field. This happens when the velocity of the particle:
explanation: When a charged particle enters a magnetic field with its velocity at an angle to the field (neither parallel nor perpendicular), the velocity component perpendicular to B causes circular motion while the component parallel to B remains unaffected. The combination of circular motion and uniform linear motion along B produces a helical (spiral) path.
topic: Force on Current and Moving Charges
subject: cuet-physics

options.text: Has components both parallel and perpendicular to B

elimination_hints.hint: When velocity is purely perpendicular to B, the particle follows a circular path, not a helical one.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing circular and helical paths

elimination_hints.hint: When velocity is parallel to B, there is no magnetic force and the particle moves in a straight line.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not recognizing that parallel motion produces zero force

elimination_hints.hint: If velocity is zero, there is no motion at all and hence no path of any kind.

subtopic: Lorentz Force and Motion in Fields

question_text: Two long parallel wires separated by a distance of 0.1 m carry currents of 5 A and 10 A in the same direction. The force per unit length between them is: (μ₀ = 4π × 10⁻⁷ T·m/A)
explanation: The force per unit length between two parallel current-carrying wires is F/L = μ₀I₁I₂/(2πd). Substituting: F/L = (4π × 10⁻⁷ × 5 × 10)/(2π × 0.1) = (4π × 10⁻⁷ × 50)/(0.2π) = (200π × 10⁻⁷)/(0.2π) = 1000 × 10⁻⁷ = 10⁻⁴ N/m = 1 × 10⁻⁴ N/m. Since the currents are in the same direction, the force is attractive.
topic: Force on Current and Moving Charges
subtopic: force between parallel currents
subject: cuet-physics

elimination_hints.hint: Check your calculation. The factor 2π in the denominator should give 10⁻⁴, not 2 × 10⁻⁴. Also, same-direction currents attract.
elimination_hints.misconception: Error in simplifying the 2π factor

elimination_hints.hint: The magnitude is correct, but same-direction currents attract each other, they do not repel.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the direction of force between parallel currents

elimination_hints.hint: This is half the correct value. Verify your substitution of I₁ and I₂ in the formula.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using incorrect current values in the formula

question_text: A proton (mass 1.67 × 10⁻²⁷ kg) moves with a speed of 5 × 10⁶ m/s perpendicular to a magnetic field of 0.5 T. The radius of the circular path described by the proton is (charge of proton = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ C):
explanation: When a charged particle moves perpendicular to a magnetic field, it follows a circular path. The radius is given by r = mv/(qB). Substituting: r = (1.67 × 10⁻²⁷ × 5 × 10⁶) / (1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ × 0.5) = (8.35 × 10⁻²¹) / (8.0 × 10⁻²⁰) = 0.104 m ≈ 0.1 m.
topic: Force on Current and Moving Charges
subject: cuet-physics

elimination_hints.hint: This is off by a factor of 10. Re-check the calculation: r = mv/(qB) = (1.67 × 10⁻²⁷ × 5 × 10⁶)/(1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ × 0.5) ≈ 0.1 m.
elimination_hints.misconception: Arithmetic error in power-of-ten calculation

elimination_hints.hint: 1.0 m is ten times the correct answer. Ensure proper handling of exponents in the numerator and denominator.
elimination_hints.misconception: Arithmetic error in power-of-ten calculation

elimination_hints.hint: 0.05 m results from an incorrect factor of 2. The formula r = mv/(qB) does not include any additional numerical factor.
elimination_hints.misconception: Introducing an extraneous factor in the formula

subtopic: Lorentz Force and Motion in Fields

question_text: The force per unit length between two long parallel conductors carrying currents I₁ and I₂ separated by a distance d is given by:
explanation: The magnetic field produced by conductor 1 at the location of conductor 2 is B₁ = μ₀I₁/(2πd). The force per unit length on conductor 2 is F/L = B₁I₂ = μ₀I₁I₂/(2πd). This expression is fundamental and is used to define the SI unit of current (ampere).
topic: Force on Current and Moving Charges
subject: cuet-physics

elimination_hints.hint: The factor is 2π in the denominator, not 4π. The field of a long wire is B = μ₀I/(2πd), and force/length = BI₂ = μ₀I₁I₂/(2πd).
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing 2π (Biot-Savart for infinite wire) with 4π (Coulomb's law constant)

elimination_hints.hint: The distance d appears to the first power, not squared. This is not an inverse-square law like Coulomb's law.
elimination_hints.misconception: Incorrectly applying inverse-square law to magnetic force between wires

elimination_hints.hint: Having d in the numerator would mean force increases with distance, which is physically incorrect. Force decreases as conductors are moved apart.
elimination_hints.misconception: Placing d in the wrong part of the formula

subtopic: Lorentz Force and Motion in Fields

question_text: An alpha particle (charge 2e, mass 4mₚ) and a proton (charge e, mass mₚ) are accelerated from rest through the same potential difference and then enter a uniform magnetic field perpendicular to their velocity. The ratio of the radii of their circular paths (rα/rₚ) is:
explanation: After acceleration through potential difference V, kinetic energy K = qV. So Kα = 2eV and Kₚ = eV. The radius of circular motion is r = √(2mK)/(qB). For alpha particle: rα = √(2 × 4mₚ × 2eV)/(2eB) = √(16mₚeV)/(2eB) = 4√(mₚeV)/(2eB). For proton: rₚ = √(2mₚeV)/(eB) = √(2mₚeV)/(eB). Therefore rα/rₚ = [4√(mₚeV)/(2eB)] / [√(2mₚeV)/(eB)] = [4/(2)] × [1/√2] = 2/√2 = √2 = 1:1 × √2. So rα/rₚ = √2.
topic: Force on Current and Moving Charges
subject: cuet-physics

elimination_hints.hint: 1:1 would require identical r values. Since the alpha particle has different mass and charge, r = √(2mK)/(qB) with K = qV gives different radii.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assuming equal radii for particles accelerated through same potential

elimination_hints.hint: 2:1 would result from r ∝ m/q without properly accounting for the velocity gained through the potential difference.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not correctly relating kinetic energy to the potential difference for different charges

elimination_hints.hint: 1:√2 is the inverse of the correct ratio. The alpha particle, with greater momentum, has the larger radius.
elimination_hints.misconception: Inverting the ratio of radii

subtopic: Lorentz Force and Motion in Fields

question_text: A proton and an alpha particle (charge 2e, mass 4mₚ) enter a uniform magnetic field perpendicular to their velocities. If both have the same kinetic energy, the ratio of the radii of their circular paths (rₚ/rα) is:
explanation: The radius of circular motion is r = mv/(qB). Since KE = ½mv², we get v = √(2KE/m), so r = m√(2KE/m)/(qB) = √(2mKE)/(qB). For the proton: rₚ = √(2mₚKE)/(eB). For the alpha particle: rα = √(2 × 4mₚ × KE)/(2eB) = 2√(2mₚKE)/(2eB) = √(2mₚKE)/(eB). Therefore rₚ/rα = [√(2mₚKE)/(eB)] / [√(2mₚKE)/(eB)] = 1. The ratio is 1:1.
topic: Force on Current and Moving Charges
subtopic: motion of charge in magnetic field
subject: cuet-physics

elimination_hints.hint: This would be correct if both had the same momentum, not the same kinetic energy. With equal KE, r = √(2mKE)/(qB).
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing equal kinetic energy with equal momentum

elimination_hints.hint: This is the inverse of option A. Neither is correct for equal kinetic energy conditions.
elimination_hints.misconception: Inverting the ratio while still using the momentum formula

elimination_hints.hint: This would arise from an error in the mass or charge ratio. For an alpha particle, m = 4mₚ and q = 2e; √(4mₚ)/(2e) = √(mₚ)/e.
elimination_hints.misconception: Algebraic error in evaluating √m/q for the alpha particle

question_text: A charged particle enters a region where a uniform magnetic field B and a uniform electric field E are both present and mutually perpendicular. If the particle has charge q, mass m, and enters with velocity v = E/B perpendicular to both fields, but the electric field is suddenly switched off, the radius of the resulting circular path is:
explanation: Initially the particle moves undeflected with v = E/B (velocity selector condition). When the electric field is switched off, only the magnetic force acts. The particle moves in a circle with radius r = mv/(qB). Substituting v = E/B: r = m(E/B)/(qB) = mE/(qB²). This radius depends on the mass-to-charge ratio and the fields.
topic: Force on Current and Moving Charges
subtopic: velocity selector
subject: cuet-physics

elimination_hints.hint: Check dimensions: mE/(qB) has units of kg·(V/m)/(C·T) = kg·m/s² × s/(C) which is not length. You need r = mv/(qB) with v = E/B.
elimination_hints.misconception: Forgetting to substitute v = E/B into the radius formula

elimination_hints.hint: This inverts E and B. The velocity is E/B, not B/E, so the radius involves E in the numerator and B² in the denominator.
elimination_hints.misconception: Inverting the velocity selector relation

elimination_hints.hint: The radius is r = mv/(qB) = m(E/B)/(qB) = mE/(qB²). There is no E² term.
elimination_hints.misconception: Squaring E instead of B in the substitution

question_text: A wire of length L is bent into a semicircular loop and carries a current I. It is placed in a uniform magnetic field B directed perpendicular to the plane of the loop. The net force on the semicircular wire is:
explanation: For a curved wire in a uniform magnetic field, the net force equals the force on a straight wire connecting the two ends of the curve. The semicircular wire of length L has radius r = L/π. The straight-line distance between the two ends (the diameter) is 2r = 2L/π. Therefore, the net force is F = BI × (2L/π) = 2BIL/π. The direction follows from F = IL × B for the equivalent straight conductor.
topic: Force on Current and Moving Charges
subject: cuet-physics

elimination_hints.hint: BIL would be the force if the entire wire length L were straight and perpendicular to B. But for a semicircle, the effective length is the chord (diameter) = 2L/π.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using the arc length instead of the chord length for net force

elimination_hints.hint: Zero net force applies to a complete closed loop in a uniform field. A semicircle is not a closed loop, so the net force is not zero.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing semicircular wire with a complete loop

elimination_hints.hint: πBIL greatly overestimates the force. The effective straight-line length is 2L/π, which is shorter than L, not longer.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using an incorrect geometric relationship

subtopic: Lorentz Force and Motion in Fields

question_text: The diagram shows a straight current-carrying conductor placed in a uniform magnetic field directed into the page. The current flows from left to right. Using Fleming's left-hand rule, the force on the conductor is directed:
image_alt: A horizontal conductor carrying current I from left to right, placed in a uniform magnetic field B directed into the page (shown by crosses). Force direction to be determined.
explanation: Using Fleming's left-hand rule: the forefinger points in the direction of the magnetic field (into the page), the middle finger points in the direction of current (left to right). The thumb then points upward, indicating the force on the conductor is directed upward.
topic: Force on Current and Moving Charges
subtopic: force on current-carrying conductor (F=BIL sinθ)
subject: cuet-physics

elimination_hints.hint: Downward would result if the current direction were reversed (right to left). Re-apply Fleming's left-hand rule with correct current direction.
elimination_hints.misconception: Reversing the current direction in Fleming's rule

elimination_hints.hint: The force cannot be along the magnetic field direction. F = IL × B is perpendicular to both I and B.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing force direction with field direction

elimination_hints.hint: The magnetic field is into the page. The force must be perpendicular to both B and I, which means it must be in the plane of the page (up or down).
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the force direction with the field's opposite direction

question_text: The diagram shows a rectangular current loop ABCD placed in a uniform magnetic field B directed along the positive x-axis. The loop carries current I in the anticlockwise direction as seen from above. The plane of the loop is in the xz-plane. The net force on the loop is:
image_alt: A rectangular loop ABCD in the xz-plane carrying anticlockwise current I. The uniform magnetic field B is along the positive x-axis. Sides AB and CD are parallel to x-axis; sides BC and DA are parallel to z-axis.
explanation: In a uniform magnetic field, the net force on any closed current loop is always zero. This is because the forces on opposite sides of the loop are equal in magnitude but opposite in direction. Sides AB and CD carry current parallel and antiparallel to B respectively, so the force on each is zero (sinθ = 0). Sides BC and DA are perpendicular to B and carry currents in opposite directions, so their forces cancel. The net force is zero, though a net torque may exist.
topic: Force on Current and Moving Charges
subtopic: force on current-carrying conductor (F=BIL sinθ)
subject: cuet-physics

elimination_hints.hint: Individual sides may experience forces, but in a uniform field, forces on opposite sides cancel exactly. The net force on a closed loop in a uniform field is always zero.
elimination_hints.misconception: Summing magnitudes of forces without considering directions

elimination_hints.hint: Forces on sides BC and DA are equal and opposite, so they cancel. The net force is zero in a uniform field.
elimination_hints.misconception: Forgetting that opposite sides carry current in opposite directions relative to B

elimination_hints.hint: No net translational force exists on a closed loop in a uniform field. A net force requires a non-uniform field.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing net force with net torque on the loop

question_text: The diagram shows two long parallel wires P and Q separated by distance d, carrying currents I₁ and I₂ respectively in the same direction (upward). The force on wire Q due to wire P is:
image_alt: Two vertical parallel wires P and Q separated by distance d. Both carry current upward. Wire P carries current I₁ and wire Q carries current I₂. The magnetic field due to P at the location of Q and the resulting force direction are to be determined.
explanation: Wire P produces a magnetic field at the location of Q. Using the right-hand rule, the field due to P at Q's location points into the page (for P on the left carrying current upward). Wire Q carries current upward in this field (into the page), so by F = IL × B, the force on Q is directed toward P (attractive). The magnitude is F/L = μ₀I₁I₂/(2πd). Parallel currents in the same direction always attract.
topic: Force on Current and Moving Charges
subtopic: force between parallel currents
subject: cuet-physics

options.text: Attractive, directed toward wire P, with magnitude μ₀I₁I₂/(2πd) per unit length
Repulsive, directed away from wire P, with magnitude μ₀I₁I₂/(2πd) per unit length
Attractive, directed toward wire P, with magnitude μ₀I₁I₂/(4πd) per unit length
Zero, because the fields from the two wires cancel

elimination_hints.hint: Same-direction currents attract, not repel. Opposite-direction currents repel. This is a fundamental result.
elimination_hints.misconception: Reversing the attraction/repulsion rule for parallel currents

elimination_hints.hint: The formula uses 2π in the denominator, not 4π. F/L = μ₀I₁I₂/(2πd) is the standard result.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the 2π factor with 4π from Biot-Savart law

elimination_hints.hint: Each wire experiences a force due to the other wire's field. The fields do not cancel at the wire locations.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing field superposition at a midpoint with force on a wire

question_text: The diagram shows a charged particle of charge +q entering a region with both electric field E (upward) and magnetic field B (into the page) with velocity v directed to the right. For what value of v will the particle travel in a straight line, and what happens if the speed is doubled?
image_alt: A positive charge +q moving to the right with velocity v. Electric field E points upward (arrows). Magnetic field B is directed into the page (crosses). The region has both fields present simultaneously.
explanation: For straight-line motion, the electric force qE (upward) must balance the magnetic force qvB (downward, by Fleming's left-hand rule for a positive charge moving right in a field into the page). So qE = qvB, giving v = E/B. If the speed is doubled to 2E/B, the magnetic force becomes 2qE (downward) while the electric force remains qE (upward). The net force is qE downward, and the particle curves downward.
topic: Force on Current and Moving Charges
subtopic: velocity selector
subject: cuet-physics

options.text: v = E/B; if doubled, the particle curves upward
v = B/E; if doubled, the particle curves downward
v = E/B; if doubled, the particle continues in a straight line
v = E/B; if doubled, the particle curves downward

elimination_hints.hint: When v > E/B, the magnetic force (downward) exceeds the electric force (upward), so the net force is downward, not upward.
elimination_hints.misconception: Reversing the direction of net force when magnetic force dominates

elimination_hints.hint: The balance condition is v = E/B, not B/E. From qE = qvB, solving for v gives E/B.
elimination_hints.misconception: Inverting the velocity selector formula

elimination_hints.hint: Straight-line motion requires exact balance qE = qvB. Doubling v breaks this balance, so the particle must curve.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking the particle remains undeflected at all speeds

question_text:
Assertion (A): The work done by a magnetic force on a charged particle moving in a magnetic field is always zero.

Reason (R): The magnetic force on a moving charged particle is always perpendicular to its velocity.
explanation: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A. The magnetic force F = qv × B is always perpendicular to the velocity v (since the cross product is perpendicular to both vectors). Work is defined as W = F · ds, where ds is along the velocity. Since F is perpendicular to v (and hence to ds), the dot product F · ds = 0 at every instant. Therefore the magnetic force does zero work. R directly explains why A is true.
topic: Force on Current and Moving Charges
subtopic: Lorentz force
subject: cuet-physics

options.text: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: R directly explains A: work = F · ds = 0 precisely because F ⊥ v. The perpendicularity is the reason for zero work.
elimination_hints.misconception: Failing to connect perpendicularity of force with zero work done

elimination_hints.hint: R is correct. The cross product v × B is always perpendicular to v by the definition of the cross product.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking the magnetic force can have a component along velocity

elimination_hints.hint: A is correct. Since F ⊥ v always, W = ∫F · ds = 0. The magnetic force cannot change the kinetic energy of a particle.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking magnetic force can do work on a charged particle

question_text:
Assertion (A): The kinetic energy of a charged particle moving in a uniform magnetic field remains constant.
Reason (R): The magnetic force on a moving charge is always perpendicular to its velocity.
explanation: The assertion is correct: since the magnetic force does no work, the kinetic energy (and hence the speed) of the charged particle remains unchanged. The reason is also correct: F = qv × B is always perpendicular to v by the nature of the cross product. Furthermore, the reason correctly explains the assertion — it is precisely because the force is perpendicular to velocity that no work is done (W = F · ds = 0 when F ⊥ v), and therefore kinetic energy is conserved.
topic: Force on Current and Moving Charges
subject: cuet-physics

options.text: Both A and R are correct and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are correct but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is correct but R is incorrect
A is incorrect but R is correct

elimination_hints.hint: R directly explains A. The perpendicularity of F to v means W = F · v dt = 0, so KE is constant. The causal link is direct.
elimination_hints.misconception: Failing to see the direct causal connection between perpendicular force and zero work

elimination_hints.hint: The reason is a fundamental property of the cross product (v × B is always perpendicular to v). R is definitely correct.
elimination_hints.misconception: Doubting the perpendicularity of the magnetic force to velocity

elimination_hints.hint: The assertion is correct — speed and hence KE of a charged particle in a magnetic field do not change. This is experimentally verified.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking magnetic field changes the kinetic energy of a charge

subtopic: Lorentz Force and Motion in Fields

question_text:
Assertion (A): A charged particle moving parallel to a magnetic field experiences no magnetic force and continues in a straight line.

Reason (R): The magnetic force on a charged particle is given by F = qE, where E is the electric field.
explanation: Assertion A is true: when a charged particle moves parallel (or antiparallel) to B, the angle θ between v and B is 0° (or 180°), so F = qvB sinθ = 0. The particle experiences no deflection and moves in a straight line. Reason R is false: the magnetic force on a moving charge is F = qv × B (Lorentz force), not F = qE. The expression F = qE describes the electric force, not the magnetic force.
topic: Force on Current and Moving Charges
subtopic: Lorentz force
subject: cuet-physics

options.text: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: R states an incorrect formula. The magnetic force is F = qv × B, not F = qE. F = qE is the electric force.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the electric force formula with the magnetic force formula

elimination_hints.hint: R is factually incorrect as it gives the electric force formula instead of the magnetic force formula.
elimination_hints.misconception: Accepting an incorrect formula as true

elimination_hints.hint: A is correct: when v is parallel to B, sinθ = 0 in F = qvBsinθ, so F = 0 and the particle is undeflected.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking a charged particle always experiences force in a magnetic field

question_text:
Assertion (A): A current-carrying closed loop placed in a uniform magnetic field experiences a net force of zero.
Reason (R): The force on a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field is given by F = I(L × B), where L is the length vector of the conductor.
explanation: The assertion is correct: for a closed loop in a uniform magnetic field, the net force is the vector sum of forces on all elements, which equals I(∮dL) × B = I × 0 × B = 0, since the line integral of dL around a closed loop is zero. The reason is also correct: F = I(L × B) is indeed the expression for force on a straight current-carrying conductor. However, the reason by itself does not directly explain why the net force on a closed loop is zero — one also needs the fact that ∮dL = 0 for a closed loop in a uniform field. So R is not the complete explanation of A.
topic: Force on Current and Moving Charges
subject: cuet-physics

options.text: Both A and R are correct and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are correct but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is correct but R is incorrect
A is incorrect but R is correct

elimination_hints.hint: While both statements are correct, R only gives the force formula for a conductor. It does not explain why the sum of all such forces on a closed loop is zero. The explanation requires ∮dL = 0.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assuming the force formula alone explains the zero net force on a closed loop

elimination_hints.hint: R is indeed correct. F = I(L × B) is the standard expression for force on a current-carrying conductor in a uniform field.
elimination_hints.misconception: Doubting the validity of the force formula

elimination_hints.hint: A is correct. A closed current loop in a uniform magnetic field has zero net force (though it may experience a torque).
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing torque with net force on a loop

subtopic: Lorentz Force and Motion in Fields

question_text:
Match Column I with Column II:

Column I:
(P) Charged particle moving parallel to B
(Q) Charged particle moving perpendicular to B
(R) Charged particle moving at angle θ (0° < θ < 90°) to B
(S) Charged particle at rest in B

Column II:
(1) Helical path
(2) Circular path
(3) Straight line (no force)
(4) No motion
explanation: P → 3: When velocity is parallel to B, sinθ = 0, so F = 0 and the particle continues in a straight line. Q → 2: When velocity is perpendicular to B, the constant perpendicular force causes circular motion. R → 1: When velocity has both parallel and perpendicular components to B, the perpendicular component causes circular motion while the parallel component causes linear drift, resulting in a helical path. S → 4: A stationary charge (v = 0) experiences no magnetic force and remains at rest.
topic: Force on Current and Moving Charges
subject: cuet-physics

elimination_hints.hint: P→2 is wrong. A particle moving parallel to B experiences zero force and moves in a straight line, not a circle.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing parallel and perpendicular motion in B

elimination_hints.hint: Q→1 is wrong. A particle moving perpendicular to B traces a circle, not a helix. A helix requires a velocity component along B.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing circular and helical paths

elimination_hints.hint: P→4 is wrong. A particle moving parallel to B is not stationary — it continues in a straight line since there is no magnetic force.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking no force means no motion

subtopic: Lorentz Force and Motion in Fields

question_text:
Match Column I with Column II:

Column I:
(P) SI unit of magnetic field
(Q) Force between two parallel conductors carrying 1 A each, separated by 1 m, per unit length
(R) Radius of circular path of charge q, mass m, speed v in field B
(S) Condition for a charged particle to pass undeflected through crossed E and B fields

Column II:
(1) mv/(qB)
(2) 2 × 10⁻⁷ N/m
(3) v = E/B
(4) Tesla (T)
explanation: P → 4: The SI unit of magnetic field is Tesla (T). Q → 2: Using F/L = μ₀I₁I₂/(2πd) with I₁ = I₂ = 1 A, d = 1 m: F/L = (4π × 10⁻⁷ × 1 × 1)/(2π × 1) = 2 × 10⁻⁷ N/m. This is the basis for the definition of the ampere. R → 1: The radius of circular motion is r = mv/(qB), obtained by equating qvB = mv²/r. S → 3: In a velocity selector, qE = qvB gives v = E/B.
topic: Force on Current and Moving Charges
subject: cuet-physics

elimination_hints.hint: Q→1 is wrong. The force per unit length between two wires is a numerical value (2 × 10⁻⁷ N/m), not the expression mv/(qB) which gives the radius of circular motion.
elimination_hints.misconception: Mixing up the radius formula with the force between parallel conductors

elimination_hints.hint: R→3 is wrong. The radius of circular path is r = mv/(qB), not v = E/B. The condition v = E/B applies to velocity selectors.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the radius formula with the velocity selector condition

elimination_hints.hint: P→2 is wrong. The SI unit of magnetic field is Tesla (4), not 2 × 10⁻⁷ N/m (which is the force per unit length between parallel conductors).
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the unit of B with the force value used in defining the ampere

subtopic: Lorentz Force and Motion in Fields

question_text: When a charged particle enters a uniform magnetic field at an angle θ (where 0° < θ < 90°) to the field direction, it follows a helical path with its axis along the magnetic field direction.
explanation: This statement is TRUE. When a charged particle enters a magnetic field at an angle θ to B, its velocity has two components: v‖ = vcosθ (along B) and v⊥ = vsinθ (perpendicular to B). The perpendicular component causes circular motion, while the parallel component causes uniform linear motion along B. The combination of circular motion perpendicular to B and linear motion along B produces a helical (spiral) path with its axis along B.
topic: Force on Current and Moving Charges
subtopic: helical motion
subject: cuet-physics

elimination_hints.hint: The velocity component along B is unaffected by the magnetic force (F = qv × B has no component along B). This component causes forward drift while the perpendicular component causes circular motion, resulting in a helix.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking the particle follows a circular path even when it has a velocity component along B

question_text: The direction of force on a current-carrying conductor placed in a magnetic field can be determined using Fleming's left-hand rule.
explanation: This statement is TRUE. Fleming's left-hand rule states: if the forefinger points in the direction of the magnetic field (B), the middle finger points in the direction of current (I), then the thumb gives the direction of force (F) on the conductor. This rule is specifically used for determining the direction of force on a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field (motor rule).
topic: Force on Current and Moving Charges
subject: cuet-physics

elimination_hints.hint: Fleming's left-hand rule is the standard rule for finding the direction of force on a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field. It is widely used and correct.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing Fleming's left-hand rule (motor rule) with Fleming's right-hand rule (generator/dynamo rule)

elimination_hints.hint: This is not a valid option for this question.
This is not a valid option for this question.

subtopic: Lorentz Force and Motion in Fields

question_text: The force between two parallel wires carrying currents in opposite directions is attractive.
explanation: This statement is FALSE. When two parallel wires carry currents in opposite directions, the force between them is repulsive, not attractive. Using the right-hand rule and F = IL × B, the magnetic field produced by one wire at the location of the other wire creates a force that pushes the wires apart. The rule is: same-direction currents attract, opposite-direction currents repel.
topic: Force on Current and Moving Charges
subtopic: force between parallel currents
subject: cuet-physics

elimination_hints.hint: Apply the right-hand rule: the field from one wire at the other wire's location, combined with the opposite current direction, gives a repulsive force. Same-direction currents attract; opposite-direction currents repel.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the rule for same and opposite direction currents

question_text: A magnetic field can change the speed of a charged particle moving through it.
explanation: This statement is FALSE. The magnetic force on a charged particle is F = qv × B, which is always perpendicular to the velocity. A perpendicular force changes only the direction of velocity, not its magnitude (speed). Since no work is done by the magnetic force (W = F · ds = 0 when F ⊥ ds), the kinetic energy and hence the speed remain constant.
topic: Force on Current and Moving Charges
subject: cuet-physics

elimination_hints.hint: The magnetic force is always perpendicular to velocity and does zero work. Since work-energy theorem gives ΔKE = W = 0, speed cannot change.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assuming any force acting on a particle changes its speed

elimination_hints.hint: This is not a valid option for this question.
This is not a valid option for this question.

subtopic: Lorentz Force and Motion in Fields

question_text: A proton (mass = 1.67 × 10⁻²⁷ kg, charge = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ C) moves in a circular path of radius 0.1 m in a magnetic field of 0.5 T. The speed of the proton is approximately ________ m/s.
explanation: For circular motion in a magnetic field: qvB = mv²/r, giving v = qBr/m. Substituting: v = (1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ × 0.5 × 0.1)/(1.67 × 10⁻²⁷) = (8 × 10⁻²¹)/(1.67 × 10⁻²⁷) = 4.79 × 10⁶ ≈ 4.8 × 10⁶ m/s.
topic: Force on Current and Moving Charges
subtopic: motion of charge in magnetic field
subject: cuet-physics

elimination_hints.hint: Check your powers of 10: (10⁻¹⁹ × 10⁻¹)/(10⁻²⁷) = 10⁻²⁰⁺²⁷ = 10⁷, not 10⁴.
elimination_hints.misconception: Error in handling powers of 10

elimination_hints.hint: The exponent should be 6, not 5. Recount: 10⁻¹⁹ × 10⁻¹ = 10⁻²⁰; dividing by 10⁻²⁷ gives 10⁷; the coefficient 8/1.67 ≈ 4.8 adjusts this to ~4.8 × 10⁶.
elimination_hints.misconception: Off-by-one error in exponent arithmetic

elimination_hints.hint: This would make the proton travel at nearly 1/6 the speed of light for a 0.5 T field and 0.1 m radius, which is too fast. Check the coefficient calculation.
elimination_hints.misconception: Incorrect division of the numerical coefficient

question_text: One ampere is defined as that steady current which, when flowing through each of two infinitely long, straight, parallel conductors placed 1 m apart in vacuum, produces a force per unit length of _____ N/m between them.
explanation: The SI definition of one ampere is based on the force between two parallel current-carrying conductors. Using F/L = μ₀I₁I₂/(2πd), with I₁ = I₂ = 1 A and d = 1 m: F/L = (4π × 10⁻⁷ × 1 × 1)/(2π × 1) = 2 × 10⁻⁷ N/m. This value defines the ampere in the SI system.
topic: Force on Current and Moving Charges
subject: cuet-physics

elimination_hints.hint: 4π × 10⁻⁷ is the value of μ₀ (permeability of free space), not the force per unit length. F/L = μ₀I₁I₂/(2πd) = μ₀/(2π) = 2 × 10⁻⁷ N/m.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing μ₀ with the force per unit length

elimination_hints.hint: 1 × 10⁻⁷ is half the correct value. The calculation gives μ₀/(2π) = 4π × 10⁻⁷/(2π) = 2 × 10⁻⁷, not 1 × 10⁻⁷.
elimination_hints.misconception: Arithmetic error in simplifying μ₀/(2π)

elimination_hints.hint: 4 × 10⁻⁷ is twice the correct value. Carefully simplify: 4π × 10⁻⁷/(2π) = 2 × 10⁻⁷.
elimination_hints.misconception: Cancellation error when dividing 4π by 2π

subtopic: Lorentz Force and Motion in Fields

question_text: In a mass spectrometer, singly ionized ions of two isotopes of an element (masses m₁ = 20 u and m₂ = 22 u, where 1 u = 1.66 × 10⁻²⁷ kg) are accelerated through a potential difference of 10 kV and then enter a uniform magnetic field of 0.2 T perpendicular to their velocity. The ions travel in semicircular paths and strike a photographic plate. What is the separation between the two lines formed on the photographic plate?
explanation: After acceleration, KE = qV, so v = √(2qV/m). The radius of the semicircular path is r = mv/(qB) = (m/(qB))√(2qV/m) = √(2mV/q)/B. The separation on the plate equals the difference in diameters: Δx = 2r₂ − 2r₁ = (2/B)√(2V/q)(√m₂ − √m₁). With q = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ C, V = 10⁴ V, B = 0.2 T: √(2V/q) = √(2 × 10⁴/(1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹)) = √(1.25 × 10²³) = 1.118 × 10¹¹·⁵. Computing: r = √(2mV/q)/B. For m₁ = 20 × 1.66 × 10⁻²⁷ = 3.32 × 10⁻²⁶ kg: r₁ = √(2 × 3.32 × 10⁻²⁶ × 10⁴/1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹)/0.2 = √(4.15 × 10⁻³)/0.2 = 0.0644/0.2 = 0.322 m. For m₂ = 22 × 1.66 × 10⁻²⁷ = 3.652 × 10⁻²⁶ kg: r₂ = √(2 × 3.652 × 10⁻²⁶ × 10⁴/1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹)/0.2 = √(4.565 × 10⁻³)/0.2 = 0.0676/0.2 = 0.338 m. Separation = 2(r₂ − r₁) = 2(0.338 − 0.322) = 2 × 0.016 = 0.032 m ≈ 3.2 cm.
topic: Force on Current and Moving Charges
subject: cuet-physics

elimination_hints.hint: 1.6 cm is the difference in radii (r₂ − r₁), not diameters. The separation on the photographic plate equals the difference in diameters: 2(r₂ − r₁).
elimination_hints.misconception: Forgetting to multiply the radius difference by 2 for the diameter difference

elimination_hints.hint: 6.4 cm is double the correct answer. Check that you are not multiplying by 4 instead of 2 when converting from radius to diameter difference.
elimination_hints.misconception: Doubling the diameter difference instead of the radius difference

elimination_hints.hint: 0.8 cm is too small. This may result from an error in the calculation of radii or forgetting to use the correct mass values.
elimination_hints.misconception: Arithmetic error in computing the radii

subtopic: Lorentz Force and Motion in Fields

scenario: A cyclotron is used to accelerate protons (mass = 1.67 × 10⁻²⁷ kg, charge = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ C). The cyclotron has a magnetic field of 1.5 T and the radius of the dees is 0.5 m. The oscillating electric field between the dees accelerates the protons each time they cross the gap.
question_text: What is the maximum kinetic energy (in MeV) that the protons can achieve in this cyclotron? (1 MeV = 1.6 × 10⁻¹³ J)
explanation: The maximum speed is achieved when the proton reaches the edge of the dee: v_max = qBR/m = (1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ × 1.5 × 0.5)/(1.67 × 10⁻²⁷) = (1.2 × 10⁻¹⁹)/(1.67 × 10⁻²⁷) = 7.19 × 10⁷ m/s. Maximum KE = ½mv² = ½ × 1.67 × 10⁻²⁷ × (7.19 × 10⁷)² = ½ × 1.67 × 10⁻²⁷ × 5.17 × 10¹⁵ = 4.32 × 10⁻¹² J. Converting to MeV: 4.32 × 10⁻¹² / 1.6 × 10⁻¹³ = 27 MeV. Alternatively, KE = q²B²R²/(2m) = (1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹)² × (1.5)² × (0.5)² / (2 × 1.67 × 10⁻²⁷) = (2.56 × 10⁻³⁸ × 2.25 × 0.25)/(3.34 × 10⁻²⁷) = 1.44 × 10⁻³⁸/(3.34 × 10⁻²⁷) ≈ 4.31 × 10⁻¹² J ≈ 27 MeV.
topic: Force on Current and Moving Charges
subtopic: cyclotron
subject: cuet-physics

elimination_hints.hint: This is half the correct answer. Ensure you are using KE = q²B²R²/(2m) and not missing any squared term.
elimination_hints.misconception: Missing the square on one of the quantities in the KE formula

elimination_hints.hint: This is double the correct answer. Check the factor of 2 in the denominator of KE = ½mv².
elimination_hints.misconception: Forgetting the factor of ½ in the kinetic energy formula

elimination_hints.hint: This is one-fourth of the correct value. Verify that you have squared both B and R in the formula.
elimination_hints.misconception: Forgetting to square the magnetic field or radius

question_text: Arrange the following steps in the correct order for determining the force on a current-carrying conductor placed in a magnetic field using the formula F = BIL sinθ.

items.text: Calculate F = BIL sinθ by substituting the known values
Identify the magnetic field strength B and current I in the conductor of length L
Use Fleming's left-hand rule to determine the direction of the force
Determine the angle θ between the current direction and the magnetic field

explanation: The correct sequence is: (Step 2) First identify the given quantities: B, I, and L. (Step 4) Determine the angle θ between the current direction and the magnetic field. (Step 1) Substitute all values into F = BIL sinθ to calculate the magnitude. (Step 3) Finally, use Fleming's left-hand rule to find the direction of the force.
topic: Force on Current and Moving Charges
subtopic: force on current-carrying conductor (F=BIL sinθ)
subject: cuet-physics

elimination_hints.hint: You cannot calculate F = BIL sinθ (step 1) before determining θ (step 4). The angle is needed for the substitution.
elimination_hints.misconception: Attempting calculation before all variables are identified

elimination_hints.hint: You should identify B, I, and L (step 2) before determining the angle (step 4), as you need to know the setup first.
elimination_hints.misconception: Starting with angle determination before understanding the physical setup

elimination_hints.hint: Fleming's rule (step 3) gives direction, which is typically determined after computing the magnitude. You also need B and I first.
elimination_hints.misconception: Determining force direction before identifying the physical quantities

question_text: Arrange the following phenomena in order of increasing magnetic force experienced, from least to greatest, for identical charged particles (charge q, mass m) moving with speed v.

items.text: Particle moving parallel to a uniform magnetic field B: F = qvB sin0° = 0
Particle moving at 30° to a uniform magnetic field B: F = qvB sin30° = 0.5qvB
Particle moving at 60° to a uniform magnetic field B: F = qvB sin60° = 0.866qvB
Particle moving perpendicular to a uniform magnetic field B: F = qvB sin90° = qvB

explanation: The magnetic force on a charged particle is F = qvB sinθ. As the angle θ between v and B increases from 0° to 90°, sinθ increases from 0 to 1. At θ = 0° (parallel): F = 0. At θ = 30°: F = 0.5qvB. At θ = 60°: F = 0.866qvB. At θ = 90° (perpendicular): F = qvB (maximum). The order from least to greatest force is: parallel (0°) → 30° → 60° → perpendicular (90°), i.e., 1 → 2 → 3 → 4.
topic: Force on Current and Moving Charges
subtopic: Lorentz force
subject: cuet-physics

elimination_hints.hint: This is the order from greatest to least, but the question asks for least to greatest (increasing order).
elimination_hints.misconception: Reversing the required ordering direction

elimination_hints.hint: sin60° > sin30°, so the force at 60° is greater than at 30°. Items 2 and 3 are in wrong order here.
elimination_hints.misconception: Incorrectly ranking sin30° and sin60°

elimination_hints.hint: A particle moving parallel to B (item 1) has zero force, which is less than force at 30° (item 2). Item 1 should come first.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking a parallel-moving particle experiences some force
